original_abstract: This article evaluates the interpretive framework for reality popularized by Keith Ward, arguing that Ward utilizes specific panentheistic criteria to bridge material and spiritual understandings of existence. Analyzing this position from a Reformational theological perspective, the study explores the underlying reasons why panentheism and a bipolar conception of God have gained significant traction within certain Christian circles. It further examines why these views are increasingly perceived as compelling alternatives to classical theism. Finally, the article offers critical recommendations for those navigating the intellectual appeal of generic Christian panentheism, particularly when it threatens to eclipse traditional doctrines of God.
humanized_abstract: In this article I evaluate the Keith Ward’s concept of the world view (i.e. world view as understood by humans). According to Ward, in the world view the criterion for assessing the qualities of the world view has been determined by panentheistic ideas. I want to assess the ideas underlying the Reformational evaluation of the panentheism and the bipolar view of God which function as a motivation in various Christian groups. I also try to clarify why these trends in the contemporary philosophy of God are taken by many to be more plausible than classical theism. At the end of this article I give a critical assessment of the intellectual challenge of the generic Christian panentheism, while also taking into account that it can be very tempting to trade the classical doctrine of God for it.
